title: Lesson 2: BLESS Rhythms - Living on Mission Every Day
description: Five practical rhythms for living on mission where you live, work, and play: Begin with Prayer, Listen, Eat, Serve, Story. Not a program - a way of life.

blocks.text: In Lesson 1 you drew your Personal Ministry Map and identified the people God has placed in your spheres of life. You know the vision: you are sent. This lesson gives you the practice. The challenge with mission is usually not motivation. Most people who have encountered Jesus want others to know Him too. The challenge is not knowing what to do on a Tuesday. BLESS is a set of five rhythms that give shape to missional living in ordinary life. They are not a program or a checklist. They are habits, the kind of habits that, practiced consistently, naturally create relationships where the gospel can take root.

blocks.alt: BLESS - Begin with Prayer, Listen, Eat, Serve, Story

blocks.text: BLESS stands for: Begin with Prayer, Listen, Eat, Serve, and Story. Each rhythm has a simple practice attached to it. None of them require a special gift or a particular personality. They just require intention, and a little courage.
B - Begin with Prayer

Every rhythm in BLESS starts here, because mission starts here. Before you can love people well, you need to see them the way God sees them, and prayer is what adjusts your vision. It is how you learn to notice people you have been walking past and discover where God is already at work before you show up. Build a short prayer list of three to five people from your Personal Ministry Map who do not follow Jesus and pray for them daily, not for a spiritual breakthrough, but for them: their health, their family, the thing you know they are carrying. Talk to God about people before you talk to people about God.
Continue steadfastly in prayer, being watchful in it with thanksgiving. At the same time, pray also for us, that God may open a door for the word, that we may declare the mystery of Christ.

2.1 - Write the names of 3-5 people from your Personal Ministry Map who do not follow Jesus. These are your people to pray for daily. What do you know about each of them that you can bring to God this week?
L - Listen

Most people do not feel heard. They go through entire days, sometimes entire seasons of life, without anyone asking them a genuine question and waiting for a real answer. When you slow down and actually listen to someone's story, their hopes, their fears, and what they are carrying, something happens. They feel seen. And people who feel seen are open in ways they normally are not. People do not care how much you know until they know how much you care.
The purpose in a man's heart is like deep water, but a man of understanding will draw it out.

2.2 - Think of someone on your prayer list. What do you actually know about their life right now, what they are carrying, hoping for, struggling with? What is one genuine question you could ask them this week to go deeper?
E - Eat

Jesus ate His way through the gospels. He ate with Pharisees and sinners, with outcasts and fishermen, with friends and with people who would betray Him. He cooked breakfast on a beach, broke bread the night before He died, and even the second coming is pictured as a feast. The Pharisees accused Him of receiving sinners and eating with them, and Jesus wore it as a description. Welcoming someone to a seat at your table shows and tells the Gospel.
Now the tax collectors and sinners were all drawing near to hear him. And the Pharisees and the scribes grumbled, saying, "This man receives sinners and eats with them."

2.3 - Who is someone on your prayer list, or someone in your neighborhood, office, or community, that you could invite to a meal or coffee in the next two weeks? Write their name and a specific plan: where, when, and what you will say when you invite them.
S - Serve

One of the most disarming things a person can experience is someone doing something for them with no strings attached, no agenda, no follow-up pitch, just help. It could be helping someone move, watching their kids for an afternoon, dropping off a meal when they are sick, or fixing the thing that keeps breaking. The smaller and more specific the act, the more it communicates that you were actually paying attention to their life. Even Jesus defined His own mission in terms of service: the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve. When you serve someone practically, you are showing them what Jesus is like before you ever say His name.
Even as the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many.

2.4 - What tangible need do you see in someone around you right now, on your street, in your building, at your workplace? What is one specific way you could show up and serve them this week, with no agenda attached?
S - Story

Every other rhythm in BLESS builds relational trust. This final rhythm is what that trust makes possible: sharing how Jesus has changed your story. Your testimony is not a doctrinal argument or a sales pitch. It is simply your experience: what your life was like before, how you encountered Jesus, and what is different now. No one can argue with that, because it is yours. Ask yourself often: does everyone who knows me know the gospel?
But in your hearts honor Christ the Lord as holy, always being prepared to make a defense to anyone who asks you for a reason for the hope that is in you; yet do it with gentleness and respect.

2.5 - Write your testimony in 3-4 sentences using this simple frame: (1) What my life looked like before Jesus, (2) how I encountered Him, (3) what is different now. Write it simply, the way you would say it to a friend, not a church crowd.
Putting It Together

BLESS is not a checklist you complete once a week. It is a set of rhythms that, over time, shape how you move through the world. Some weeks you will be heavy in Prayer and light in Serve. Some seasons a single relationship will move through all five over the course of a year. The goal is not to do all five every day. The goal is to have them become so natural that when you walk out your front door, you are already asking: who am I praying for, who do I need to listen to, who can I eat with, who can I serve, whose story is intersecting with mine?
2.6 - Which of the five BLESS rhythms comes most naturally to you? Which one is hardest? What is one thing you will do this week to practice the hardest one?

2.7 - Look back at your prayer list from question 2.1. For each person, write which BLESS rhythm is the most natural next step: do they need you to listen more, eat with them, serve them in a specific way, or share your story? Make a plan for this week.
Closing Reflections
What is one key thing you learned from this lesson, and what next step can you take this week?
What have you learned about God and yourself recently in your Daily Devotions?
What is a prayer request for this week?
